projects.title: Angular AI Copilot Starter
projects.problem: Most frontend portfolios do not prove streaming AI UX, cited responses, and safe action flows in one coherent artifact.
projects.solution: Build a local-first Angular demo with streaming chat, RAG citation cards, tool timeline, and human approval states.
projects.recruiterTakeaway: This project proves the candidate can design AI product interfaces beyond a basic chatbot shell.
interviewTalkingPoints: Partial token rendering without layout jank
How citations signal trust and confidence
How to represent tool execution visibly

projects.title: UI-Aware Agent Demo
projects.problem: Most AI demos show a chat panel, not an agent participating in the visible workflow.
projects.solution: Build a demo with context inspector, suggested actions, approval checkpoints, and recovery states.
projects.recruiterTakeaway: This proves the candidate understands agent-aware product UX and operational legibility.
interviewTalkingPoints: Difference between chat UI and UI-aware agents

projects.title: Enterprise Agent Approval Workflow
projects.problem: Enterprise AI tooling needs governance-grade action review, not only delight-driven UI.
projects.solution: Build an approval queue, risk tiers, reviewer roles, and an audit log around simulated agent actions.
projects.recruiterTakeaway: This proves enterprise judgment, not only frontend polish.
interviewTalkingPoints: What makes approval UX different from a confirm dialog
How to classify action risk tiers
How audit trails affect interface design
How to keep governance flows usable

githubPlan.headlineRewrite: AI Frontend Architect building Angular and TypeScript interfaces for AI copilots, RAG citation workflows, tool execution UX, approval-safe agentic systems.

readmeChecklist.item: Lead with what the project proves, not just the stack.
Show one screenshot, GIF, or concrete visual in the first screen.
Add a 'mocked vs real' section that is unambiguous.
Explain architecture decisions in recruiter-readable language.
Add a roadmap that separates built work from planned work.
Make local setup copy-paste simple and verified.

interviewMap.proofPrompt: Prepare one real example that proves streaming response ux.
Prepare one real example that proves rag citation trust states.
Prepare one real example that proves tool-call timeline design.
Prepare one real example that proves human approval before risky actions.
Prepare one real example that proves enterprise-safe agent interfaces.

interviewMap.topic: Moving from mocks to real backends
interviewMap.proofPrompt: Prepare one real example that proves moving from mocks to real backends.

weeklyPlan.weeklyTheme: Convert current frontend experience into visible AI proof.
outcomes: Write one interview-safe explanation of a real decision.

dailyPlan.task: Rewrite the GitHub profile README and summary so AI frontend architecture is visible in the first screen.
dailyPlan.proofArtifact: Target role is explicit in public positioning

dailyPlan.task: Rewrite one project summary so it states what the project proves.
Capture one screenshot, GIF, or annotated walkthrough for the flagship project.
Draft one STAR story from a real project decision.
Add a mocked-vs-real section and recruiter takeaway to the flagship README.

truthfulnessGuardrails: Do not claim production usage unless the public artifact proves it.
Label mock services as mock services in every README and demo.
Separate built evidence from planned roadmap work.
Do not invent metrics, user counts, stars, or contribution outcomes.
Only publish claims you can defend in a recruiter screen or interview.
comparison: Use occupation-ops when the main problem is weak public proof before applying.
Use job-search pipeline tools when the main problem is scanning, tracking, or operationalizing active applications.
Occupation-ops is not a tracker, scanner, or mass-apply workflow; it is a proof engine for candidate credibility.
